---
title: "Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint"
chapter_id: "context-transactions-snapshots-mounts-and-taint"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "vcm_public"
  - "ladon_manhattan"
  - "context_engineer"
  - "black_hole_context_manager"
  - "vcm_editable"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Four local raw-cache mappings are passage-reviewed; `vcm_editable` remains connector/source-note mapped, and support remains argument until memory-store, branch-isolation, deletion-closure, or taint conformance tests exist."
  - "Only protocol schema/fixture validation is implemented; the fixture now records transaction state, transaction validity state, snapshot boundary, mount policy, isolation state, taint propagation, rollback/deletion closure, declassification refs, derivatives, materialization state, consumer policy, verification refs, promotion blockers, replay boundary, context ABI refs, source refs, support-state effect, and non-claims, but no transactional memory-store tests have been run for this chapter."
  - "Lean coverage is limited to finite event/snapshot and taint-propagation records; memory-store, branch-isolation, and deletion-closure behavior remain unimplemented."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `context-transactions-snapshots-mounts-and-taint` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `vcm_public`, `ladon_manhattan`, `context_engineer`, `black_hole_context_manager`, `vcm_editable` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `vcm_public`; supporting: `ladon_manhattan`, `context_engineer`, `black_hole_context_manager`; connector/recovery: `vcm_editable` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `vcm_public`, `ladon_manhattan`, `context_engineer`, `black_hole_context_manager`, `vcm_editable`; passage-reviewed local raw cache: `vcm_public`, `ladon_manhattan`, `context_engineer`, `black_hole_context_manager`; connector/source-note only: `vcm_editable` |
| Test state | Context transaction fixture validation now includes transaction state, transaction validity state, snapshot boundary, mount policy, isolation state, taint propagation, rollback/deletion closure, declassification refs, derivative refs, materialization state, consumer policy, verification refs, promotion blockers, replay boundary, context ABI refs, source refs, support-state effect, and non-claims; `AsiStackProofs.ContextTransactions` finite-record proofs are implemented; memory-store tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats context transactions as a required runtime contract, not as an implemented database. The repo validates a transaction record fixture and implements narrow finite-record Lean predicates for committed-event snapshot reads and taint propagation without declassification. It does not yet implement a memory store, mounts, branch isolation, deletion closure, or copy-on-write branches.

It follows the certificate chapter because typed cells still need runtime semantics. Provenance and loss contracts are not enough if the memory view is stale, mixed across branches, or derived from tainted material.

This chapter is where context stops being a bag of retrieved text and becomes state. A stateful memory layer has to answer what view was read, what changed, what was hidden, what was tainted, what was deleted, and what derivative obligations now exist.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

Typed context cells still need runtime semantics. This chapter treats context like a transactional view: it can be mounted, branched, tainted, committed, rolled back, expired, declassified, or denied. Without those semantics, even well-labeled memory can become stale, mixed across incompatible tasks, or derived from material that should no longer be used.

The human analogy is version control for what the model gets to see. If a future reviewer cannot tell which memory view was read, which branch it came from, what was private, what was tainted, and what derivatives now carry obligations, the system cannot honestly explain its own reasoning surface.
:::

## Problem

Memory and context need runtime semantics for consistency, branches, contradiction, deletion, privacy, and taint.

The previous chapter gave context representations certificates. This chapter gives the memory system runtime semantics. Long-horizon work needs snapshots that are coherent enough to replay, mounts that define what memory is visible, branches that support experiments, taint that follows sensitive or suspect sources, and deletion closure that prevents derived summaries from resurrecting removed data.

VCM supplies the transaction model. Context Engineer and Ladon add need-to-know and sensitive-mount pressure. Black Hole Context Manager adds practical memory-budget machinery: chunks, drift, mass, freezing, eviction, and critical context preservation.

The central problem is not only "which context is relevant?" It is "which context view is legally, epistemically, temporally, and authority-wise valid for this task?" A high-quality retrieval hit can still be invalid if it came from a revoked source, an expired snapshot, an unauthorized mount, or a tainted derivative.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A retrieval store cannot guarantee read-your-writes behavior, copy-on-write experimentation, deletion closure, or taint propagation by itself.

Retrieval can return plausible neighbors, but it does not by itself define consistency. A worker may read a mixture of old and new facts, see a source after revocation, miss a contradiction, or consume a summary derived from deleted data. Security labels can also evaporate when context is copied, summarized, embedded, logged, or sent to a tool.

The stack needs database-like discipline without pretending memory is a database table. Context objects are semantic, versioned, compressed, mounted, and task-relative. That makes transaction semantics more important, not less.

Long-context windows can hide this problem by making more material visible at once. More visibility is not the same as a coherent view. A transcript that mixes old summaries, fresh evidence, private notes, and revoked derivatives can be larger while becoming less governable.

## Core Claim

[context-transactions-snapshots-mounts-and-taint.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] VCM should use transactional memory semantics: immutable events, versioned pages, snapshots, mounts, taint, temporal validity, and deletion closure.

The claim remains at `argument` support. VCM public v1 supplies snapshot, revocation, invalidation, protected compilation, and audit vocabulary. Ladon/Manhattan and Context Engineer supply compartmentalization and need-to-know pressure. Black Hole Context Manager supplies chunk mass, drift, freezing, and eviction patterns. The editable VCM source refines terminology; it is not a local memory-store implementation.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C maps this transaction claim to all assigned source notes. Four local raw-cache mappings are now passage-reviewed. The mappings support transaction vocabulary, snapshot and invalidation boundaries, sensitive-compartment lifecycle patterns, taint/deletion pressure, and context-budget mechanisms, not a production memory store, branch-isolation harness, deletion-closure result, security guarantee, or benchmark result. `vcm_editable` remains connector/source-note mapped until durable raw text, memory-store artifacts, conformance results, VCM-Bench records, or external corroboration are imported or inspected.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `vcm_public` | Passage-reviewed: object/version/representation/validation/packet/materialization identities, role-indexed obligations, representation graphs, protected compilation, semantic faults, runtime materialization keys, core-vs-transactions separation, immutable versions, pinned snapshots, commit/merge/branch/invalidation/deletion-closure ownership, race-free invalidation, residue reports, semantic page state, atomic memory transactions, causal ordering, copy-on-write branches, cache invalidation, task checkpoints, execution classes, and write-time controls. | No local memory store, resolver, deletion-closure harness, branch-isolation test, VCM conformance run, or independent interoperability result is present. |
| `ladon_manhattan` | Passage-reviewed: agency/credential exposure pressure, blind execution, kernel-level enclave/vault framing, opaque handles, trusted credential-entry overlay, syscall interception, permission-table checks, secret injection, Digital SCIF spawn/inject/execute/wipe/return lifecycle, side-channel defense considerations, and Rust implementation sketch. | No Ladon implementation, kernel test, side-channel validation, security audit, or theorem proof exists here. |
| `context_engineer` | Passage-reviewed: layered memory, context shards, clearance labels, memory masks, allowed tools, Digital SCIFs, ephemeral injection, zeroization, sanitized commits, information-flow controls, outgoing secret scans, source-reported benchmark figures, and limitations around software-only isolation and summarization loss. | No runtime enforcement or benchmark reproduction for clearance, taint, leak prevention, cost, or latency exists here. |
| `black_hole_context_manager` | Passage-reviewed: chunk records, embeddings, entropy, cached mass, timestamps, goal-vector state, lazy reweighting under goal drift, HMAC and multi-confirmation promotion gates, dormant cluster freezing, disk swap/thaw, tiered cache budgeting, mass-based eviction, TTL downgrade, hot/warm/cold cache framing, and a proposed drifting-needle scenario. | No package, local test run, freeze/evict fixture, benchmark, or production-behavior evidence exists here. |
| `vcm_editable` | Connector/source-note mapped: stable addresses, mounts, snapshots, materializations, semantic paging, protected compilation, authority non-escalation, and transactional/governed memory vocabulary. | Connector-readable variant only; no local VCM-Bench, memory-store conformance run, or model-facing result exists. |

## Mechanism

Certificates make context units honest; transactions make memory operations replayable. VCM public v1 supplies immutable versions, snapshot coherence, revocation, invalidation, protected compilation, and audit records. Ladon/Manhattan and Context Engineer supply sensitive mounts, opaque handles, ephemeral compartments, zeroization, clearance labels, memory masks, and sanitized commits. Black Hole Context Manager contributes chunk mass, drift, freezing, eviction, and critical-context preservation. Editable VCM ties those ideas back to mounts, snapshots, materializations, semantic paging, protected compilation, and transactional memory.

A Context Transaction Record is the runtime ledger for reads, writes, branches, taint propagation, deletion obligations, contradiction records, and faults.

The reviewed passages sharpen the runtime boundary. VCM public v1 justifies transaction vocabulary, snapshot coherence, invalidation, deletion-residue reporting, copy-on-write branches, execution classes, and write-time controls. Ladon/Manhattan justifies keeping sensitive authority behind handles and ephemeral compartments. Context Engineer justifies clearance-labeled mission contexts, memory masks, Digital SCIF zeroization, sanitized commits, and leak-scanning pressure. Black Hole Context Manager justifies memory-budget machinery around chunks, mass, drift, freezing, eviction, and critical-context preservation. None of those passages show this repository running a memory store, enforcing mount visibility, proving branch isolation, closing deletion over derivatives, validating side-channel defenses, or reproducing source-reported benchmark numbers.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Immutable memory event"] --> B["Versioned page"]
  B --> C["Mount policy"]
  C --> D["Snapshot"]
  D --> E["Read / write / derive"]
  E --> F{"Branch or commit?"}
  F -- "branch" --> G["Copy-on-write branch"]
  F -- "commit" --> H["Committed event"]
  E --> I["Taint + deletion closure"]
  G --> I
  H --> I
  I --> J["Derivative refs + declassification refs"]
  J --> K{"Closure satisfied?"}
  K -- "yes" --> L["Materializable view"]
  K -- "no" --> M["Fault / quarantine"]
```

The transaction model records immutable memory events, compiles task snapshots with mounts and copy-on-write branches, and propagates taint plus deletion obligations through every derived context unit. A materialized view is acceptable only when its read set, write set, branch policy, transaction validity state, isolation state, taint labels, deletion obligations, declassification references, derivative references, contradiction references, materialization state, closure state, faults, consumer policy, verification refs, promotion blockers, replay boundary, non-claims, and audit references are coherent enough to replay.

The record should distinguish view construction from view use. View construction decides mounts, snapshot, clearance, branch, and materialization. View use records which cells were actually read, which derivatives were produced, which taint labels propagated, and whether any deletion or declassification obligation was triggered. If the view becomes a durable artifact, the artifact should point back to the transaction rather than merely naming the source files it happened to read.

Immutable events make audit and replay possible. Versioned pages make supersession explicit. Snapshots give a task a coherent read view. Mounts define what can be seen and written. Copy-on-write branches let the system test alternate summaries, repairs, or plans without mutating the committed memory space.

Taint should propagate through derived cells unless authorized declassification occurs. Deletion should close over derivatives or fault when closure cannot be proven. Contradictions should become evidence records, not overwritten memory.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Context Transaction Record.

- Planning receives consistent views.
- Security labels sensitive mounts.
- Evidence records contradictions and supersession.

Minimum fields:

- `transaction_id`
- `transaction_state`
- `transaction_validity_state`
- `operation`
- `snapshot_id`
- `snapshot_boundary`
- `mounts`
- `mount_policy`
- `read_set`
- `write_set`
- `branch_policy`
- `isolation_state`
- `taint_labels`
- `taint_propagation`
- `deletion_obligations`
- `rollback_or_deletion_closure`
- `declassification_refs`
- `derivative_refs`
- `contradiction_refs`
- `materialization_state`
- `closure_state`
- `faults`
- `context_abi_refs`
- `source_refs`
- `audit_refs`
- `consumer_policy`
- `verification_refs`
- `promotion_blockers`
- `replay_boundary`
- `support_state_effect`
- `non_claims`

Planning receives consistent views. Security labels sensitive mounts and tainted derivatives. Evidence records contradictions, supersession, invalidation, deletion closure, declassification decisions, materialization state, and faults.

The interface should also make failed materialization ordinary. A context compiler that cannot prove mount visibility, snapshot coherence, taint propagation, or deletion closure should emit a typed fault that planning can route around. Silent best-effort context is the unsafe fallback.

## Invariants

- Snapshots are consistent.
- Taint propagates to derivatives.
- Deletion closure is enforced or faulted.
- Materialization state is explicit before a view is used by a downstream artifact or job.

The deletion invariant is strict: deleted or revoked source material cannot reappear through derived summaries unless a policy explicitly says what remains permitted. If closure cannot be established, the system should fault rather than materialize the view.

Another invariant is branch containment. Experimental summaries, speculative repairs, and alternate plans can branch, but they do not become committed memory unless merge policy, authority, taint, contradiction, and residual checks pass.

## Failure modes

- Memory poisoning.
- Stale or contradictory reads.
- Deleted data reappearing in derived summaries.

Memory poisoning enters through malicious, stale, or over-weighted chunks. Stale or contradictory reads produce incoherent plans. Deleted data reappearing in summaries creates legal, privacy, and trust failures. These are transaction faults, not mere retrieval imperfections.

The subtle failure is context laundering. A sensitive or unsupported source can be summarized, embedded, cached, or merged until its original restrictions disappear. Transaction records should keep the restriction attached to every derivative or force an explicit declassification decision.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

A transaction model for context reads, writes, branches, and deletion closure.

The smallest useful implementation is a `context_transaction_record` schema and fixture. It validates transaction state, transaction validity state, operation, snapshot, snapshot boundary, mounts, mount policy, read/write sets, branch policy, isolation state, taint labels, taint propagation, deletion obligations, rollback/deletion closure, declassification refs, derivative refs, contradiction refs, materialization state, closure state, faults, context ABI refs, source refs, audit refs, consumer policy, verification refs, promotion blockers, replay boundary, support-state effect, and non-claims. The accompanying Lean module is intentionally narrower than a store: it models finite committed events, snapshot views, and derived taint records; a valid snapshot read exposes a committed event in the declared view; and tainted sources produce tainted derivatives unless declassification is authorized.

The fixture validates only the record surface. The Lean predicates do not prove read-your-writes consistency across a store, branch isolation, deletion closure, or poisoning resistance.

The next useful implementation step is a tiny memory-store fixture set: one coherent snapshot read, one branch that cannot merge because taint remains, one deleted source whose derivative blocks materialization, one unauthorized mount fault, one declassification record that preserves residual risk, and one artifact graph node that records the replay boundary. That would test transaction semantics without claiming production memory behavior.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature version is a transactional memory substrate for AI context. It does not treat memory as a vector store with annotations. It treats every context view as the product of committed events, versioned pages, mount policies, snapshots, branch rules, taint propagation, deletion obligations, declassification decisions, and replay boundaries.

Under transactional context memory, every materialized context view can explain itself. It can show which immutable events were visible, which branch was used, which mounts were readable or writable, which cells were actually read, which derivatives were created, which taint labels propagated, which deletion obligations closed, which contradictions were recorded, and why a view was allowed to feed a job, artifact, claim, or release.

The final product surface would include:

- A context event store with immutable writes, versioned pages, pinned snapshots, copy-on-write branches, and merge policies.
- Mount and isolation policies for public memory, private notes, source caches, secret handles, Digital SCIF material, and task-local scratch space.
- Taint propagation and declassification records that follow sensitive, suspect, private, revoked, or unsupported material through every derived cell.
- Deletion-closure and revocation checks that fault when deleted material survives in summaries, embeddings, artifacts, or cached packets.
- Replay and audit tools that reconstruct a context view and its derivative obligations before a claim or artifact is promoted.

This governable-memory state layer remains a target architecture, not a current-result claim. Support should stay at `argument` until memory-store behavior, mount visibility, branch isolation, deletion closure, taint propagation, and replay tests show that the stack can branch, summarize, and forget without losing obligations attached to what it read.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Context transaction fixture validation | Check that the transaction fixture matches the public schema and declares transaction state, transaction validity state, operation, snapshot boundary, mounts, mount policy, read/write sets, branch policy, isolation state, taint propagation, deletion obligations, rollback/deletion closure, declassification refs, derivatives, materialization state, contradictions, closure state, faults, context ABI refs, source refs, audits, consumer policy, verification refs, promotion blockers, replay boundary, support-state effect, and non-claims. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Snapshot read predicate | Check that a valid finite snapshot read sees a committed event in its declared view. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.ContextTransactions`; builds locally |
| Taint propagation predicate | Check that tainted source context produces tainted derived context unless declassification is authorized. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.ContextTransactions`; builds locally |
| Deletion closure test | Check that deleted or revoked source material cannot reappear through derivatives without a closure record. | planned; not run |
| Memory-store conformance test | Check read-your-writes, branch isolation, mount visibility, and committed-event replay in an executable store harness. | planned; not run |

The fixture-shape check and finite-record proof predicates are implemented. Store consistency, branch isolation, and deletion closure require a small memory-store harness with committed events, derived cells, declassification policy, and deletion/revocation cases.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:vcm.transactions.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.ContextTransactions` | A snapshot read sees the committed events in its declared view. | implemented |
| `lean:vcm.transactions.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.ContextTransactions` | A tainted source produces tainted derived context unless declassification is authorized. | implemented |

These proof hooks are implemented only over finite Lean event/snapshot and taint records. `AsiStackProofs.ContextTransactions` checks that valid snapshot reads expose committed events in their declared view, and that tainted sources taint derived context unless declassification is authorized. The proof does not implement a transactional memory store, branch isolation, mount visibility, deletion closure, or poisoning resistance.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `vcm_public` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1 | memory_context | Public VCM release. Governed protocol for compiled working context. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `ladon_manhattan` | Ladon & The Manhattan Protocol | security_governance | Kernel-level security architecture for high-agency AI. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `context_engineer` | Context Engineer / Manhattan Protocol | memory_context_lineage | Context as supply chain; compartmentalized information logistics. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `black_hole_context_manager` | Black Hole Context Manager | memory_context_lineage | Earlier context management spec with lazy evaluation, batching, drift checks, security hardening. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `vcm_editable` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1.0_Editable | memory_context | Editable version with evidence-carrying planner-guided context compiler framing. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The transaction and taint sources carry the runtime semantics for context state, memory budgets, and deletion obligations. Four mappings have passage review, but the core claim stays at `argument` because `vcm_editable` remains connector/source-note mapped and memory-store behavior, mount visibility, branch isolation, deletion closure, side-channel validation, context-manager execution, and benchmark claims remain unpromoted.

## Summary

Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint gives VCM runtime semantics. It treats memory as immutable events, versioned pages, coherent snapshots, mounted views, copy-on-write branches, tainted derivatives, deletion obligations, contradiction records, and auditable faults.

The rule is that memory operations must leave a reviewable trace. If a context view cannot satisfy consistency, taint, or deletion closure, it should fault before becoming working context. The next chapter asks a separate question: even when the context view is coherent and authorized, is it adequate for the claim the system is about to make?

The practical standard is that context must fail closed. A system that cannot explain the view it read should not use that view as if it were ordinary memory.
